tweets.text:
Mira Murati says human-AI collaboration needs models that can listen while they think:

"The types of models that we work with today, they're very turn-based. You talk, they talk, then they go off and think."

"While they're thinking, it's almost like they're deaf and blind. They cannot perceive anything else about what's going on."

"By contrast, our interactions with each other are very rich. There is a lot of information in our interactions when we are silent, when we're thinking, when we're interrupting one another."

"Interaction models are able to capture all of this nuance. They're not turn-based. They're more like time-based interaction, where they're continuously taking in audio, text, video, and continuously providing output."

"This enables you to catch things like interruptions and simultaneous speech, and really create a rich, high bandwidth interaction between humans and machines."

@miramurati at Bloomberg Tech live with @emilychangtv

tweets.text:
Mira Murati says frontier AI should be built like a tandem bike:

"Having humans in the loop doesn't quite describe it because it sounds like a checkpoint where we're signing off something, and then you're good to go."

"It's more like creating systems that are not just autonomously advancing and leaving civilization behind, but are more like a tandem bike."

"When you're going up a hill, maybe whoever is stronger is pedaling harder. But both hands are on the wheel. That's quite important because that's a different system. It's a system designed for collaboration."

"It will increase the level of agency that people have, and also it will help us steer the research direction towards creating outputs that are more value-aligned."

@miramurati at Bloomberg Tech live with @emilychangtv

tweets.text:
I expect the May CPI print on June 10th to run hot, north of 4% on the headline, and then I think we start seeing the headline number come down.

Based on the data in front of me, headline CPI starts to come down with the June print that lands in July.

I do not think the Fed will raise rates this year. 

CPI climbed to 3.8% last month from 3.3% in March, and energy drove 40% of that acceleration. 

An oil supply shock is stagflationary. It lifts prices while pulling growth lower at the same time. Hiking into that does nothing to the cause and only deepens the damage on the other side. 

I think that is the logic the Fed will lean on to keep its hands off the trigger. 

The only thing that creates inflation is printing money. A supply shock moves relative prices. It does not create sustained inflation m/m forever, which is why the Fed can look straight through it. Everything else is anecdotal at best. 

And remember - The one fact pertaining to all conditions is that they will change.
